findings.title: _priceAtCanonical bypasses effective staleness gate via canonical offset
findings.description: _priceAtCanonical walks back PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS (7) days from canonicalDate, but canonicalDate itself may already be backward-snapped from the intended entryDate. For a US holiday like MLK Day, computeBenchmarkReturn returns canonicalEntry = entryDate-4 (Friday before Monday holiday). _priceAtCanonical then walks back another 7 days from that Friday, reaching entryDate-11. The intended staleness gate (no price older than 7 days from targetDate) is silently violated: a ticker whose last trade was 11 calendar days before the entry date is included instead of being dropped. In contrast, priceAtLegacy enforces an explicit _daysBetween(chosen.date, targetDate) > PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS check that would correctly return null.
findings.mechanism: computeBenchmarkReturn calls nearestTradingDay(entryDate, SPYmap) which may snap backward by N days to find the nearest SPY close. The resulting canonicalEntry is then passed to _priceAtCanonical. _priceAtCanonical walks back PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS from canonicalEntry, not from the original entryDate. Effective staleness tolerance = benchmark_snap_offset + PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS, which can reach 4+7=11 days for US market holidays.
findings.reproducer: Run on a vintage dated the Tuesday after MLK Day (3rd Monday of January). entryDate = asOf+1 (pre-market). nearestTradingDay(entryDate=Tuesday, SPYmap) exact-hits Tuesday. But if entryDate is a holiday Monday itself: canonicalEntry = Friday(-3). Any pick with last price on the preceding Monday(-10) passes the _priceAtCanonical 7-day walkback from Friday but would be dropped by priceAtLegacy which checks distance from the original Monday.
findings.impact: Thinly traded foreign stocks or stocks around US public holidays contribute stale entry prices to pick return calculations. This inflates or deflates pick returns relative to the benchmark which uses a clean close. The bias direction depends on whether stale-price stocks performed above or below average in the intervening days.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a final staleness check in _priceAtCanonical against the original intended targetDate (passed as the unused fallbackTargetDate parameter): after finding a price, assert _daysBetween(foundDate, originalTargetDate) <= PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS, else return null. Alternatively pass the original asOf/entryDate to _priceAtCanonical and enforce the check there.

findings.title: frozenVintageMedianReturn benchmark includes the picks being measured
findings.description: computeFrozenVintageMedianReturn builds its benchmark universe from ALL tickers across ALL modes in picksFile.modes. When evaluateVintage then computes alphaVsFrozenVintage for HYPERGROWTH, the benchmark already contains all HYPERGROWTH picks. The alpha formula is median(HYPERGROWTH_returns) - median(ALL_mode_picks_including_HG). This makes the benchmark self-referential: any outperformance by HYPERGROWTH inflates the benchmark median, suppressing observed alpha. It does NOT measure 'how much better did HYPERGROWTH do vs a neutral universe' — it measures 'how much better did HYPERGROWTH do vs the average of all selected modes'. For 2026-05-08: HG=28 picks, QC=65 picks; frozen universe = 93 tickers, 30% of which are the very HG picks being evaluated.
findings.mechanism: tickersAtVintage (line 231) iterates Object.values(vintagePicks.modes) and adds ALL tickers from ALL modes. The frozen benchmark median is then a weighted average of ALL mode returns. When this is subtracted from mode-specific pickMed to get alphaVsFrozenVintage, the contamination from the target mode's own picks pulls the benchmark toward the target, systematically understating alpha.
findings.reproducer: Any vintage with multiple modes. HYPERGROWTH has 28 picks, QUALITY_COMPOUNDER has 65. frozenVintageMed = median(93 picks). HG alpha = median(28) - median(93). If QC performs poorly (dragging down the 93-pick median), HG alpha is inflated. If QC performs well, HG alpha is deflated. Neither reflects HYPERGROWTH alpha vs a neutral universe.
findings.impact: alphaVsFrozenVintage is a misleading metric surfaced in the methodology-report.md. Decisions to promote or retire modes based on this alpha are biased by cross-mode contamination. The metric appears in walk-forward.json and walk-forward.md as if it is a clean alternative benchmark.
findings.suggested_fix: For mode M, compute the frozen-vintage benchmark from only tickers that appear in OTHER modes (not M): tickersAtVintage.delete each ticker in the current mode's picks before computing benchmark returns. Or rename the metric to 'intra-mode relative alpha' and document that it is NOT a neutral alpha.

findings.title: Survivor bias in method-effectiveness: delisted fail-group tickers dropped silently
findings.description: method-effectiveness computes alpha = median(pass_returns) - median(fail_returns). Tickers that were delisted between the vintage date and the forward-return date have no exit price in prices/history.json and are dropped from BOTH groups (line 281-288: ret=null -> continue). Delisted tickers are disproportionately those that declined sharply before delisting. If a method correctly predicted a future loser (pass=false), that loser is removed from the fail group. The fail group's median return IMPROVES, artificially shrinking the spread between pass and fail. Alpha is inflated for any method that correctly screens out eventual delistings. The imbalance warning (line 313) logs when dropout counts differ between pass and fail, but this is post-hoc logging that does not affect the alpha calculation.
findings.mechanism: Tickers with no price map OR no valid return at exitDate are incremented into droppedTickers and excluded from bucket.pass / bucket.fail. The exclusion is symmetric in code but asymmetric in outcome: delisted stocks cluster in the fail bucket (methods correctly rejected them) but the crash returns that justify the rejection are excluded. This biases alpha upward.
findings.reproducer: Take any method that identifies financial distress signals. Stocks that fail the method (pass=false) are more likely to be distressed and delist. Their large negative returns are missing from failArr. median(failArr) improves, alpha = median(passArr) - median(failArr) shrinks toward zero or inflates.
findings.impact: Method effectiveness rankings overstate alpha for any method with distress-prediction power. Evidence gate (CI lower-bound > 0) may fire for methods whose positive alpha is entirely attributable to deleted fail-group delistings. Retirement decisions for underperforming methods are undermined.
findings.suggested_fix: For each dropped ticker with a known delisting date, impute a -100% return (worst-case) and include it in the appropriate bucket. Alternatively, record a delisting indicator in prices/history.json and substitute -100% for all tickers with delisting date < exitDate. At minimum, add a per-vintage attrition ratio to the output JSON so consumers can see what fraction of the fail group was dropped.

findings.title: getEntryDate UTC=21 threshold is DST-unaware, adds spurious 1-day entry delay in summer
findings.description: getEntryDate uses a hardcoded threshold of UTC hour < 21 to detect 'pre-market' snapshots and shift entry to T+1. US market closes at 16:00 ET. During EDT (UTC-4, approximately March–November), 16:00 ET = 20:00 UTC. A snapshot at 20:30 UTC after EDT close has full post-market price data available, but getEntryDate treats it as pre-market (20 < 21) and shifts entry to T+1. All 7 pre-market vintages in picks-history are affected (the 2026-05-09 22:15 UTC vintage is the only exception). For a global portfolio with Asian and European tickers, the same T+1 shift is applied regardless of whether those markets had already closed at snapshot time.
findings.mechanism: d.getUTCHours() < 21 evaluates to true for UTC hour 20 (post-EDT close). After EDT adjustment, market close = 20:00 UTC. A snapshot at 20:30 UTC has today's close prices but is incorrectly classified as pre-market, causing entry to be shifted to T+2 (from the market's perspective: T+1 from today's close date).
findings.reproducer: Run snapshot-picks.js on any weekday between March and November between 20:00 and 21:00 UTC. asOf will be that day, getEntryDate returns asOf+1. Entry price is T+1's close — one full trading day after the data was actually available.
findings.impact: Entry prices are from the day AFTER the screener ran, adding spurious slippage to backtest returns. This makes backtested alpha appear slightly lower than achievable execution. Not look-ahead bias (conservative direction), but 7 out of 8 current vintages are affected, making all current walk-forward statistics systematically delayed. For Asian/European picks, shifting to T+1 US day leaks next-day non-US price movements into the entry price.
findings.suggested_fix: Account for DST: during EDT (UTC-4) use threshold 20, during EST (UTC-5) use 21. Or use a fixed UTC offset for the US Eastern close: closeUTC = 20 if isDST else 21. Simple DST heuristic: month >= 3 and month <= 11 -> EDT (20:00), else EST (21:00).

findings.title: 5 of 8 vintages missing evaluatedTickers: universe alpha is null for 62% of history
findings.description: evaluatedTickers (the list of all stocks in the universe at snapshot time) was first added in the 2026-05-14 vintage. All vintages from 2026-05-08 to 2026-05-13 (5 of 8 = 62.5%) have no evaluatedTickers field. For these vintages, walk-forward-perf.js cannot compute alphaVsUniverse (line 436: finalAlphaVsUniverse = null). The walk-forward summary medianAlphaVsUniverse is computed from only 3 vintage alpha values. With n=3, the median is a single value (the middle of 3 values) and is not statistically meaningful, yet is presented in walk-forward.json without any n-warning analogous to the per-vintage MIN_SAMPLES gate.
findings.mechanism: snapshot-picks.js line 275 was added to save evaluatedTickers. Historical vintages written before this change lack the field. walk-forward-perf.js detects absence via line 341 and sets missingEvaluatedTickers=true, nulling out alphaVsUniverse for the entire vintage. The summary aggregation at line 553 calls median() on however many non-null values remain — currently 3.
findings.reproducer: Run walk-forward-perf.js against current picks-history. Check outputs/walk-forward.json modes[*].summary['28d'].medianAlphaVsUniverse — it is based on at most 3 data points (May 14, 15, 17).
findings.impact: The medianAlphaVsUniverse in walk-forward.json appears as a precise number (e.g., +2.31pp) but is derived from 3 vintages. Decisions based on this metric (methodology-report.md promotion/retirement) may act on noise. Additionally: for walk-forward vintages where alphaVsUniverse = null, the 'alpha' key in the output also = null (same value per line 447), making the per-vintage 'alpha' field uninformative for the majority of historical vintages.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a vintageCountForUniverse counter to the summary and gate medianAlphaVsUniverse display/use on a minimum count (e.g., MIN_VINTAGES=4 matching method-effectiveness). Alternatively, surface the 'n=' count alongside the metric in methodology-report.md so consumers can judge statistical weight.

findings.title: method-effectiveness too-early gate uses asOf not realEntryAsOf for 28d/84d check
findings.description: The too-early gate at line 175-177 computes futureDate = WF.addDaysIso(asOf, days) and skips if futureDate > today. But the ACTUAL exit date used for price lookup is realFutureDate = WF.addDaysIso(realEntryAsOf, days) where realEntryAsOf = getEntryDate(asOfTs) = asOf+1 for pre-market snapshots (which is 7 of 8 current vintages). For a pre-market vintage, realFutureDate = asOf + days + 1. The gate allows processing when asOf+days <= today, but the actual exit lookup is at asOf+days+1. If today = asOf+days exactly, the gate passes, the vintage is processed, and all exit prices are null (tomorrow's date has no prices yet). All returns are null, n=0, alpha=null. The vintage wastes computation and a confusing warning about 0 data points may be generated.
findings.mechanism: getEntryDate(asOfTs) adds 1 day for pre-market snapshots. The too-early gate does not account for this shift. For a vintage exactly at the horizon boundary (asOf+28 = today), the gate says 'ok' but realFutureDate = today+1 has no prices. result is a zero-return vintage that consumes time loading the methods-history file and populating cacheEntries with null returns.
findings.reproducer: Vintage with asOf='2026-04-21' (pre-market), days=28. Today=2026-05-19. Gate: WF.addDaysIso('2026-04-21',28) = '2026-05-19' <= '2026-05-19' -> proceeds. realEntryAsOf='2026-04-22'. realFutureDate='2026-05-20'. All exit prices null. n=0 for every method.
findings.impact: Wasted computation and confusing log output (0 returns processed, n=0 imbalance warnings). Cache entries written with all-null returns are stale and waste cache space. No data corruption — all alphas remain null for this vintage.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the gate to: const gateFutureDate = WF.addDaysIso(WF.getEntryDate(file.generatedAt || asOf + 'T00:00:00Z'), days); if (gateFutureDate > today) continue; This requires loading the file before the gate, but avoids wasting computation on inevitable no-data vintages.

findings.title: walk-forward summary medianAlphaVsBenchmark mixes vintages with different benchmark tickers
findings.description: computeBenchmarkReturn selects the first available of SPY/QQQ/IWM. If prices/history.json later loses SPY data (e.g., price-pull failure), subsequent vintages use QQQ. The summary aggregation at line 555 (medianAlphaVsBenchmark) collects alphaVsSpy from ALL vintages regardless of which benchmark was used. SPY and QQQ have different compositions (S&P 500 vs Nasdaq-100) and different volatility profiles. Averaging alpha vs SPY and alpha vs QQQ as if they were the same benchmark overstates precision. The per-vintage benchmarkTicker field exists in the per-vintage output but is not propagated to the summary stats.
findings.mechanism: collect('alphaVsSpy') aggregates the benchmarkReturn-relative alpha for all 'ok' vintages. There is no sub-aggregation by benchmarkTicker. median() is applied to the mixed population.
findings.reproducer: Run walk-forward-perf.js twice: once with SPY in history.json, once with SPY removed. The second run produces QQQ-relative alpha. If both outputs are merged or compared, the medianAlphaVsBenchmark values are not comparable.
findings.impact: methodology-report.md shows medianAlphaVsBenchmark as a single authoritative number. If the benchmark composition changed mid-history (due to price-pull gaps), the metric silently reflects a mix. Investment decisions based on this metric are unreliable when benchmark continuity breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Track benchmark ticker in summary: for each horizon, collect vintages grouped by benchmarkTicker and compute separate medians. Surface as medianAlphaVsBenchmarkBySym: {SPY: ..., QQQ: ..., IWM: ...}. At minimum, add a field benchmarkMix: {SPY: N, QQQ: M} to the summary to show if mixing occurred.

findings.title: horizonActualDays computed but never stored in output; window normalization is dead code
findings.description: computeBenchmarkReturn calculates horizonActualDays (the actual calendar-day count between canonical entry and exit dates, e.g., 82 days when backward snap shortens a nominal 84d horizon). The comment at lines 284-290 explicitly states this was added so cross-vintage alpha aggregation can normalize windows. However, horizonActualDays is returned in the benchmark result but is NEVER included in the per-vintage horizonResults output (lines 438-468). It is also never used in the summary aggregation. Comparing a 79-day return vintage against an 84-day return vintage in the same medianAlphaVsBenchmark aggregate adds unmeasured noise, particularly for the 84d horizon where a long US holiday weekend can shorten the window by 5 days.
findings.mechanism: benchResult.horizonActualDays is available in benchByHorizon[days] but the per-vintage horizonResults object at line 438 does not include a horizonActualDays key. The summary aggregation at line 555 uses the mixed-window alpha values directly with no weighting or normalization by actual window length.
findings.reproducer: Run walk-forward-perf.js and inspect outputs/walk-forward.json. Check any vintage's modes[*].vintages[*].horizons['84d'] — horizonActualDays field is absent. Confirm benchByHorizon is computed but its horizonActualDays key is discarded.
findings.impact: Cross-vintage alpha comparisons for the 84d horizon include a systematic noise source proportional to the variance in actual window lengths (typically 79-84 days). The effect is strongest for strategies with annualized volatility > 40% where a 5-day window difference produces ~1pp return noise. This partially offsets or inflates alpha estimates at 84d.
findings.suggested_fix: Add horizonActualDays: benchResult.horizonActualDays to the horizonResults output object. In summary aggregation, optionally annualize returns before taking median (divide return by horizonActualDays, multiply by 365) for consistent cross-vintage comparison.

findings.title: Method-effectiveness cache has no invalidation on price-history changes
findings.description: The cache at outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json stores computed per-ticker return percentages (ret) keyed by (vintage_date, horizon). Once cached, these ret values are replayed forever until the 90-day prune evicts them. If prices/history.json is updated with corrected price data (e.g., a corporate action adjustment, data vendor correction, or split retroactive fix), the cached ret values remain stale. No cache version key, no hash of prices/history.json, and no option to force-invalidate exist.
findings.mechanism: Cache check at line 181: if (cachedDates.has(cacheKey) && cache.vintageReturns[cacheKey]) replay from cache without re-reading prices. loadJson(PRICES_PATH) is called once at startup (line 110), building priceIndex. But cached vintages never consult priceIndex at all — they replay stored ret values regardless of priceIndex content.
findings.reproducer: Correct a price error in prices/history.json for a ticker present in a cached vintage. Run method-effectiveness.js. The corrected price is ignored; the stale return from the cache is used. The alpha for methods containing that ticker is not updated.
findings.impact: A price correction (e.g., a major corporate action missed by the data vendor) can permanently corrupt method effectiveness scores for any vintage within the 90-day retention window. The error propagates silently with no warning.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pricesFileHash field to the cache (SHA-256 of the first N bytes of prices/history.json or its mtime). On cache load, compare current hash to stored hash. If different, invalidate the cache and rebuild. Alternatively, add a --force-rebuild CLI flag.

findings.title: Regime tagging uses vintage asOf date, not entryDate; 30-day lookback masks recent transitions
findings.description: macroRegime = getRegimeAt(regimes, asOf) uses the vintage's asOf date (the snapshot date) rather than the entryDate (the actual trade entry date, which is asOf+1 for 7/8 current vintages). The regime alpha breakdown in the summary aggregation (lines 543-546) then groups vintages by the asOf regime. For a pre-market snapshot, this tags the regime 1 day early. Additionally, getRegimeAt has a 30-day lookback: if no regime entry exists within 30 days, it returns the most recent regime found. This means a regime transition that occurred 15 days ago still labels ALL recent vintages with the OLD regime. A BULL->BEAR transition would show all recent entries as BULL-regime trades even though they entered a BEAR market.
findings.mechanism: getRegimeAt walks back up to 30 days (line 313-320). If the regime data file is not updated frequently (e.g., weekly), any transition within the lookback window is invisible — all vintages in the window get the pre-transition regime tag. The console warning fires (line 321) only when NO regime is found within 30 days, not when the found regime is stale relative to a known transition.
findings.reproducer: Regime transitions from BULL to BEAR. Last regime data entry was 20 days ago (BULL). Walk-forward run today: getRegimeAt(today) finds 20-days-old BULL entry. Recent vintages are tagged BULL. If they were actually BEAR entries, regimeAlpha['BULL'] is contaminated with bear-market trades.
findings.impact: Regime-conditional alpha analysis (the regimeAlpha breakdown in methodology-report.md) produces misleading results when regime data is stale or when transitions occur within the 30-day lookback window. BULL regime alpha can appear lower than actual because BEAR-market trades are included in the BULL bucket.
findings.suggested_fix: Tag regime using entryDate not asOf (change line 337 to getRegimeAt(regimes, entryDate)). Reduce lookback to 7 days (1 trading week) to avoid masking transitions. Add a staleness warning when the found regime entry is more than 7 days old.

findings.title: snapshot-picks result.asOf uses wall clock, not RUN_DATE_UTC; filename/firstSeenAt mismatch
findings.description: snapshot-picks.js uses process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC (or new Date()) for 'today' (controlling firstSeenAt and weeksOnList). But result.asOf = new Date().toISOString() (line 220) always uses the wall clock. The vintage filename = result.asOf.slice(0,10). If RUN_DATE_UTC='2026-05-13' (workflow pinned date) but the actual wall clock is 2026-05-14 (job ran just after midnight UTC), the output file is picks-history/2026-05-14.json with firstSeenAt='2026-05-13' for all new picks. walk-forward-perf.js reads asOf from the file content (not the filename) and will use 2026-05-14T00:00:xx as the vintage date, computing getEntryDate as 2026-05-15 (pre-market). But the data was evaluated as of 2026-05-13.
findings.mechanism: RUN_DATE_UTC controls the business-logic 'today' for enrichment but result.asOf = new Date().toISOString() always reflects wall clock. The divergence occurs when a GH Actions workflow runs after midnight UTC (common for overnight scheduled runs).
findings.reproducer: Set RUN_DATE_UTC=2026-05-13 and run snapshot-picks.js at 00:05 UTC on 2026-05-14. Output file: picks-history/2026-05-14.json. asOf=2026-05-14T00:05:xx.xxxxZ. firstSeenAt for new picks: 2026-05-13. Walk-forward reads asOf=2026-05-14, shifts entry to 2026-05-15 (pre-market). Effective entry is 2 days after the intended evaluation date.
findings.impact: Entry date for midnight-straddle vintages is off by 1 extra day, reducing measured alpha by one additional day of holding. firstSeenAt metadata is inconsistent with the vintage date. Over many midnight-straddle runs, entry date drift accumulates systematic noise in the backtest.
findings.suggested_fix: Use process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC for result.asOf when set: result.asOf = (process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC ? process.env.RUN_DATE_UTC + 'T00:00:00.000Z' : new Date().toISOString()). This ensures the vintage date, filename, asOf field, and firstSeenAt are all consistent.

findings.title: walk-forward summary has no MIN_VINTAGES gate; medianAlphaVsUniverse from 3 data points shown as authoritative
findings.description: method-effectiveness.js gates alpha output behind MIN_VINTAGES=4 (line 48). walk-forward-perf.js has no equivalent gate in the summary aggregation. As of 2026-05-19, only 3 vintages (May 14, 15, 17) have evaluatedTickers, making medianAlphaVsUniverse a median of 3 values (the middle value). For alphaVsBenchmark, all 8 vintages contribute, but 8 data points of 7-day to 84-day forward returns that are all 'too-early' for 84d and 28d means only 7d vintages contribute, and with today=2026-05-19, only vintages up to 2026-05-12 have computable 7d returns (asOf+7 <= today). None of the current vintages qualify for 7d either since all asOf dates are 2026-05-08 to 2026-05-17. The summary shows medianAlpha values that are computed from an empty set (null) or from 3 non-null values without flagging insufficient statistical power.
findings.mechanism: median([]) returns null. median([a, b, c]) returns b. These are presented identically in the output JSON with no vintageCount context for the universe-alpha specifically. The vintageCount field counts total 'ok' status vintages, not those with non-null alpha.
findings.reproducer: Run walk-forward-perf.js with current picks-history. inspect outputs/walk-forward.json modes.HYPERGROWTH.summary['28d'] — medianAlphaVsUniverse will be null (all 28d horizons too early) or based on very few points. vintageCount will show total 'ok' vintages, which is misleading because most have null universe alpha.
findings.impact: methodology-report.md may display medianAlphaVsUniverse as a concrete number (or '—') without context. If the consumer interprets a non-null number as statistically meaningful, they may make mode weighting decisions based on 1-3 data points.
findings.suggested_fix: Add universeAlphaVintageCount to the summary (count of non-null alphaVsUniverse values). Add a gate: medianAlphaVsUniverse = vintages_with_non_null_universe_alpha >= MIN_VINTAGES_FOR_SUMMARY ? computed_median : null. Define MIN_VINTAGES_FOR_SUMMARY=4 (matching method-effectiveness).

findings.title: method-effectiveness canonical fallback requires both canonical dates in ticker's map; asymmetric with walk-forward
findings.description: In method-effectiveness.js, the canonical path requires: map.has(canonicalEntry) AND map.has(canonicalExit). If either canonical date is absent from the ticker's price map, the code falls back to per-ticker nearestTradingDay for BOTH entry and exit. In contrast, walk-forward-perf.js uses _priceAtCanonical for each date independently, which walks back from canonicalDate up to 7 days. This inconsistency means method-effectiveness uses a wider variety of effective entry/exit date pairs than walk-forward. For the same ticker+vintage, method-effectiveness may use a different return window than walk-forward, making cross-comparison of alpha metrics inconsistent.
findings.mechanism: The guard (map.has(canonicalEntry) && map.has(canonicalExit)) at line 271 requires exact matches for both dates. walk-forward's _priceAtCanonical(map, canonicalEntry) accepts any price within 7 days of canonicalEntry. A ticker trading on entryDate-2 (not exactly on canonicalEntry) would: be included in walk-forward (via _priceAtCanonical walkback) but fall back to per-ticker snap in method-effectiveness.
findings.reproducer: A ticker that trades on Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri but not Wednesday. canonicalEntry = Wednesday (US holiday snap to Tuesday? No, backward would find Tuesday). Actually: canonicalEntry is from SPY snap, which trades daily. canonicalEntry is an actual trading day. Ticker that doesn't trade on canonicalEntry falls back to per-ticker in method-effectiveness but gets included via _priceAtCanonical in walk-forward.
findings.impact: Different tickers are included/excluded in walk-forward vs method-effectiveness for the same vintage, making the two analyses non-comparable. A ticker included in walk-forward alpha but excluded from method-effectiveness (using per-ticker snap instead) may have different pass/fail characteristics than the shared canonical window picks.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the guard in method-effectiveness.js with _priceAtCanonical calls: p0 = WF._priceAtCanonical(map, canonicalEntry); p1 = WF._priceAtCanonical(map, canonicalExit); if (p0 != null && p1 != null) use them, else use per-ticker fallback. This aligns the inclusion logic with walk-forward.

findings.title: _priceAtCanonical fallbackTargetDate parameter declared but never used
findings.description: _priceAtCanonical is declared as function _priceAtCanonical(map, canonicalDate, fallbackTargetDate). The fallbackTargetDate parameter is never referenced in the function body. The function comment (lines 178-181) references a 'canonical date anchoring' design but the third argument was apparently intended as an additional fallback date if the 7-day walkback from canonicalDate fails. Currently if canonicalDate + 7-day walkback returns null, the function returns null without trying fallbackTargetDate. All 4 callers pass only 2 arguments.
findings.mechanism: JavaScript ignores extra undefined parameters. fallbackTargetDate is always undefined at runtime. The function silently omits the fallback logic it declares to accept.
findings.reproducer: Call _priceAtCanonical(map, '2026-01-17', '2026-01-21') for a ticker with no prices between 2026-01-10 and 2026-01-21 (e.g., halted for 11 days). Function returns null (correct), but would return the price from 2026-01-10 if fallbackTargetDate were used to widen the search window.
findings.impact: Some tickers are dropped from calculations that could be included. Effect is conservative (drops uncertain data) but may reduce sample size for thinly traded tickers. Inconsistency between documented API and actual behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Either remove the parameter from the signature (document that null is returned if canonical + 7-day walkback fails) or implement the fallback: if the 7-day walkback from canonicalDate returns null AND fallbackTargetDate is provided, call nearestTradingDay(fallbackTargetDate, map) as a final fallback.

findings.title: Bootstrap CI percentile index is off-by-one at upper bound
findings.description: The 95% CI percentile bootstrap uses: lo = alphaSamples[Math.floor(alphaSamples.length * 0.025)] and hi = alphaSamples[Math.floor(alphaSamples.length * 0.975)]. For 200 samples: lo = index 5 (6th element = 3.0th percentile), hi = index 195 (196th element = 97.75th percentile). Standard 95% CI percentile method uses indices 4 (2.5th) and 194 (97.5th). The current formula shifts the upper bound one sample outward, making the CI slightly wider than nominal 95% (approximately 95.5%). The lower bound is also slightly above 2.5th percentile, making it marginally conservative.
findings.mechanism: Math.floor(200 * 0.975) = Math.floor(195.0) = 195. Correct index for 97.5th percentile with 200 samples is 194 (0-indexed). The formula consistently produces hi = alphaSamples[195] instead of alphaSamples[194].
findings.reproducer: Call bootstrapAlphaCI(passArr, failArr, 200) and compare returned hiCI against manually computed 97.5th percentile. The returned hi will be the 196th ranked value, not the 195th.
findings.impact: The upper CI bound is systematically 0.5% too wide. The evidence gate (ciLo95 > 0) uses the lower bound, which is slightly conservative (good). The upper bound being slightly too wide means ciHi95 < 0 (negative alpha detection) is slightly harder to reach, mildly protecting against false-negative-alpha flags. Net effect: very minor, direction is conservative.
findings.suggested_fix: Use: hi = alphaSamples[Math.ceil(alphaSamples.length * 0.975) - 1] or equivalently alphaSamples[Math.round(alphaSamples.length * 0.975) - 1]. For 200 samples: Math.ceil(195.0) - 1 = 194. Consistent with standard percentile interpolation.

findings.title: methodology-report.js reads non-existent wf.benchmarkSymbol field, always shows 'Benchmark'
findings.description: methodology-report.js line 60: const benchSymbol = (wf.benchmarkSymbol || 'Benchmark'). walk-forward.json does not contain a top-level benchmarkSymbol field; its benchmarks field is an array ['SPY-preferred', 'QQQ-fallback', ...]. The fallback 'Benchmark' is used in every run. Table column headers in the methodology-report.md always read 'α vs Benchmark' instead of 'α vs SPY' (or whichever ticker was actually used). Users reading the report cannot determine the benchmark from the report alone.
findings.mechanism: wf.benchmarkSymbol is undefined at runtime. The || 'Benchmark' fallback fires on every invocation.
findings.reproducer: Run methodology-report.js after walk-forward-perf.js. Open outputs/methodology-report.md. Column header shows '| α vs Benchmark |' not '| α vs SPY |' even though all vintages used SPY.
findings.impact: Reduced readability and transparency. No calculation error. Users may not realize the benchmark was SPY vs QQQ, which matters for interpreting alpha magnitude (QQQ has higher returns than SPY historically, so α vs QQQ would be smaller).
findings.suggested_fix: In walk-forward-perf.js, add benchmarkSymbol to outJson: benchmarkSymbol: (['SPY','QQQ','IWM'].find(t => priceIndex[t]) || null). In methodology-report.js, the existing logic then correctly surfaces the actual ticker.

findings.title: walk-forward console output displays universe-relative alpha labeled as plain 'alpha'
findings.description: The main() function console output at lines 617-619 reads: 's28.medianAlpha' which is the deprecated alias for medianAlphaVsUniverse (line 561). The log line prints e.g. 'HYPERGROWTH 4w median α = +2.31pp (n=3)' where alpha is vs universe median, not vs SPY/QQQ/IWM. A developer reading the logs without inspecting walk-forward.json would assume the alpha is benchmark-relative (SPY) when it is actually universe-relative. The two measures can differ significantly, especially when the universe has a strong upward bias.
findings.mechanism: data.summary['28d'].medianAlpha = median(collect('alphaVsUniverse')) (line 561). Console.log uses this as '4w median α' without qualifying which benchmark it is relative to.
findings.reproducer: Run walk-forward-perf.js. Observe console output line 'X 4w median α = Ypp'. Compare to walk-forward.json where modes[X].summary['28d'].medianAlphaVsBenchmark may be significantly different.
findings.impact: Misleading operator-visible output. No data corruption. Risk of operational confusion when comparing console alpha to dashboard alpha (which shows alphaVsSpy).
findings.suggested_fix: Change console.log to explicitly label the benchmark: console.log('  ' + mode + ' 4w median α vs universe = ' + ...) or switch to medianAlphaVsBenchmark for the console output.

methods/runner.js (out of scope, referenced in snapshot-picks.js)
